title: Whole-decoder stage breakdown for ranker context
hypothesis: Before choosing a combined producer/ranker RTL target, the exploration should expose a rough whole-decoder breakdown across attention, MLP, output projection, and ranker. The dominant stage may change under streaming-weight versus resident-weight memory assumptions, especially at long context.

direct_comparison.primary_question: Which decoder stage dominates latency and traffic across GPT-2-like and larger proxy shapes when sequence length, compute throughput, memory bandwidth, and weight residency are varied?
include: attention QKV, score, value, and output projection terms

expected_benefit: prevents over-optimizing ranker-local points before seeing whole-decoder dominance
shows when attention/KV traffic becomes the main frontier
sets the next RTL target in context of decoder-level latency and traffic
risks: stage timing is an analytical bound, not measured RTL
memory hierarchy is represented only by bandwidth and weight-residency cases
ranker is approximated as a standalone streaming bound until reused producer-integrated PPA is folded in

required_evaluations.objective: Estimate a coarse whole-decoder single-token latency and traffic breakdown across attention, MLP, output projection, and ranker.

expected_result.reason: The expected output is a decoder-level dominance map used to choose whether the next RTL job should target ranker integration, output projection service, or attention/KV movement.
